danger_signals.visual_form: Rolling brown/dark sprites moving down platforms with physics variation (straight or alternating left-right)
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent collision hazard; player must avoid or use hammer to destroy

danger_signals.signal: Donkey Kong's position at top of stage
danger_signals.visual_form: Large antagonist sprite positioned at apex of level structure
danger_signals.meaning: Source of barrel threats; visual anchor for threat origination

danger_signals.visual_form: Orange/red animated sprites on specific girder sections
danger_signals.meaning: Static environmental hazard; contact causes damage/loss of life

danger_signals.visual_form: Hammer sprite becomes unavailable after timer expiration (visual disappearance)
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of temporary invulnerability state; return to vulnerable status

safety_signals.visual_form: Hammer sprite appears on platform, distinguished visual asset
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability period activated; player can destroy barrels without collision damage

safety_signals.visual_form: Horizontal beam structures in gray/metallic tones
safety_signals.meaning: Safe traversal paths; structural navigation routes away from ground-level threats

safety_signals.visual_form: Female character sprite at top platform, typically stationary
safety_signals.meaning: Level objective; reaching this location completes stage

safety_signals.visual_form: Vertical climbing paths rendered as distinct sprites
safety_signals.meaning: Safe vertical mobility; alternate elevation access routes

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinctive hat, small jumping humanoid sprite
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Can occupy any platform; interacts with hammers and ladders
Stationed at level apex; barrel-throwing animation indicates aggression state

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Female character sprite; visually distinct from Mario
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Confined to top platform; immobile placement signals rescue objective

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Background girder arrangement changes; different platform layouts and fire positions per level
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barrel throw patterns and frequency scale with stage difficulty; Donkey Kong AI adjusts projectile timing

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hammer sprite visible on platform until collision or timer expiration; visual indicator of invulnerability window
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barrels destroyed on contact instead of causing collision damage; Mario movement behavior unchanged but interaction outcome differs

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character sprites transition to ending sequence; visual state shift from gameplay to cutscene
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Input freeze; automatic animation sequence begins (Mario and Pauline sitting sprites, though unused in final version)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple barrel sprites visible simultaneously on screen; increased sprite saturation as level difficulty increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barrel spawn rate increases; multiple projectiles active in later stages creates spatially complex threat environment

environmental_storytelling.example: Girder/platform layout communicates industrial setting (construction/factory environment); vertical stacking suggests building ascent progression
Fire placement on specific platforms suggests environmental danger independent of antagonist; creates multi-threat scenario requiring route planning
Pauline positioned at unreachable apex with Donkey Kong between her and player; spatial arrangement communicates obstacle and objective simultaneously
Color and size differences immediately identify Mario (player), Donkey Kong (threat), and Pauline (objective) without UI or text
Absence of 50m level in NES port (memory limitations) is narrative gap; later 'Original Edition' restoration implies design intent

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Revision 0 cannot move when D-Pad held diagonally; Revision 1 implements directional priority allowing continued horizontal movement when rolling diagonally upward
Cannot jump while holding hammer (Revision 0 bug allows rare jump under specific RNG conditions); hammer timer resets if bug triggered, extending invulnerability window
Two standard patterns: straight descent or alternating left-right lateral movement; unused third pattern (down-right trajectory) suggests design iteration
Physics-based movement through platform gaps; collision with horizontal surfaces produces bounce/ricochet behavior
Can jump while holding hammer under rare RNG alignment; unintended state reveals control interpretation ambiguity in original code

notes: Donkey Kong NES is a constrained port of the 1981 arcade original, missing the 50m (Cement Factory) level due to cartridge memory limits. The game uses simple color-coding and sprite distinctiveness rather than UI elements to communicate world state. Danger is primarily signaled through projectile visibility and spatial threat density. Two revisions exist with notable controller input and demo mode differences. The 2010 'Original Edition' Virtual Console release attempted arcade fidelity restoration. Visual communication relies entirely on sprite animation and level layout; no dynamic environmental feedback beyond projectile behavior and hazard placement. Faction identity is established purely through color palette and sprite silhouette rather than symbolic markers.
